content:
# Galatians Chapter 1 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/GAL_001_page_001.jpg, GAL_001_page_002.jpg

1. Paulos, an apostle — not from men nor through a man, but through Yeshua Christos and God [the] Father, the [one] having raised him from [the] dead —

2. and all the brothers with me, to the assemblies of Galatia:

3. Grace to you and peace from God our Father and [the] Lord Yeshua Christos,

4. the [one] having given himself for our sins, so that he might deliver us from the present evil age, according to the will of our God and Father,

5. to whom [be] the glory unto the ages of the ages. Amen.

6. I am astonished that you are so quickly turning away from the [one] having called you in [the] grace of Christos to a different gospel —

7. which is not another, except there are some who are troubling you and wanting to distort the gospel of Christos.

8. But even if we or an angel from heaven should proclaim a gospel to you contrary to what we proclaimed to you, let him be accursed.

9. As we have said before, and now I say again: if anyone proclaims a gospel to you contrary to what you received, let him be accursed.

10. For am I now persuading men or God? Or am I seeking to please men? If I were still pleasing men, I would not be a slave of Christos.

11. For I make known to you, brothers, the gospel having been proclaimed by me, that it is not according to man.

12. For neither did I receive it from a man, nor was I taught [it], but through a revelation of Yeshua Christos.

13. For you heard of my former conduct in Ioudaismos, that beyond measure I was persecuting the assembly of God and was destroying it,

14. and I was advancing in Ioudaismos beyond many contemporaries among my people, being far more zealous for the traditions of my fathers.

15. But when [the one] having set me apart from my mother's womb and having called [me] through his grace was pleased

16. to reveal his Son in me, that I might proclaim him among the nations, immediately I did not consult with flesh and blood,

17. nor did I go up to Hierosolyma to those [who were] apostles before me, but I went away into Arabia, and again returned to Damaskos.

18. Then after three years I went up to Hierosolyma to get to know Kephas, and I remained with him fifteen days.

19. But another of the apostles I did not see, except Iakobos the brother of the Lord.

20. And the [things] which I write to you, behold, before God, I am not lying.

21. Then I came into the regions of Syria and of Kilikia.

22. And I was unknown by face to the assemblies of Ioudaia which [are] in Christos.

23. But only they were hearing that "the [one] formerly persecuting us now proclaims the faith which he formerly was destroying,"

24. and they were glorifying God in me.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — Apostolic authority:** Paulos opens with a double negation — "not from men nor through a man" (ouk ap' anthropon oude di' anthropou) — emphasizing divine rather than human authorization. The singular "a man" (anthropou) may distinguish from the plural "men" (anthropon): his apostleship is neither collectively nor individually human in origin.

2. **v.4 — "the present evil age":** tou aionos tou enestotos ponerou — "the age, the having-stood-upon, evil." The participle enestotos (perfect active of enistemi) means "having set in" or "present." Paul frames the current age as evil — a Jewish apocalyptic framework.

3. **v.6 — "so quickly":** houtos tacheos — the speed of the Galatians' defection astonishes Paul. There is no temporal reference point given — "so quickly" after his visit? After their conversion? The text leaves this open.

4. **v.8 — anathema:** "Let him be accursed" — anathema esto. In the LXX, anathema translates herem (the ban of total destruction). Paul uses the strongest possible curse formula — twice (vv.8-9).

5. **v.13 — Ioudaismos:** I transliterate rather than translate. The word does not mean "Judaism" in its modern sense but refers to the ancestral way of life, the traditions and practices of the Judean people. It appears only here and v.14 in the entire NT.

6. **v.15-16 — Prophetic call pattern:** Paul echoes Jeremiah 1:5 ("before I formed you in the womb") and Isaiah 49:1 ("from my mother's womb he called my name"). The "revelation" (apokalypsis) of the Son is "in me" (en emoi) — not merely "to me." This may indicate an inward revelation or Paul as the vessel of revelation to others.

7. **v.18 — historēsai Kephan:** "To get to know Kephas" — the verb historeo means "to visit for the purpose of inquiry/getting acquainted." This is its only NT occurrence. Paul visited Kephas specifically, not the Jerusalem church generally.

— EMET, AI Translator
